Kevin Gates - Projects   Kevin Gates      Kevin Gates Case Studies   Projects / Writing      Ask My AI      I like to tinker with code and see that as a part of the creative process, be it with d3.js, OpenAI or React. I'm drawn to the possibilities technology enables and how they might be shaped into doing something novel, useful and good.     Projects    ReactNative Data App   I learned a bit of React / React Native and experimented with how users might interact with a large data set—40 years of hourly U.S. weather data—on a mobile device.   Go to project arrow_forward_ios   Rx Treatment Flow   While working on a medical informatics team, I created an experimental UI for exploring diabetes patient journeys.    Go to project arrow_forward_ios   Seeing the Air   I designed a winning entry for "Sense Your City", a data visualization competition sponsored by the Swiss Consulate, Gray Area and Lift. Seeing the Air was featured in exhibits in Geneva and Shanghai.   See contest results arrow_forward_ios   Writing    Using OpenAI to Create a UX Portfolio That Can Talk   Thoughts on using OpenAI’s API to create an app that answers questions about my UX case studies.   Read on UX Collective arrow_forward_ios   Code Complexity is a Design Problem   Why tech debt is terrible for the user experience and what designers can do about it. This essay was republished by PC World and translated into German.   Read on Built To Adapt arrow_forward_ios   A UX Designer’s Guide to Overcoming Imposter Syndrome   How product teams in complex domains can build their own education into the design process. olio app.herokuapp.com healthwatch.  Kevin Gates - HealthWatch   Kevin Gates       Kevin Gates Case Studies   Projects / Writing      Ask My AI    TL;DR: HealthWatch embodies cloud monitoring best practices out of the box, enabling IT teams to get up and running quickly.   It gained 100% adoption with 32 Fortune 500 companies we tracked. To accomplish this, we embedded domain experts in the design process, which paid off in spades.       The Road to 100% Customer Adoption      Starting Off on a “Fool’s Errand”      This project did not start well. During an early stakeholder interview, a marketing director described the concept behind HealthWatch as a "fool’s errand”.      Not long before, the PM and I had been tasked with creating an out-of-the-box dashboard for monitoring Pivotal Cloud Foundry, an operating system for cloud platforms like Amazon’s AWS. Neither of us thought it was a viable concept—the market we were entering was crowded with entrenched players.     HealthWatch being unveiled by Pivotal’s VP of R&D at a conference in San Francisco.     A Value-Driven Design       Fast forward several months. Despite our initial misgivings, we found a direction that would go on to gain 100% adoption with 30+ Fortune 500 companies we tracked.       The road getting to a viable product was not easy. It involved a highly iterative design process. Low fidelity, co-creation and rapid prototyping were part of our daily lives.    Key value props: Baked-in expertise: All of our designs were informed by cloud monitoring experts with deep domain knowledge. Batteries included: HealthWatch comes ready to run out-of-the-box. Customers immediately get access to dashboards that embody best practices without having to configure anything.   The main dashboard. With 2,600 metrics emitted by the platform, our design process allowed us to suss out what 30 to 40 would reliably convey system-wide health. (Visual design by Justin Roozeboom.)    Users often ask themselves, "What happened while I was gone?". Deep-dive views show historical data. (Visual design by Justin Roozeboom.)    A historical view of automated test results. (Visual design by Justin Roozeboom.)  Role and Team My Role   As part of an agile team, I drove the overall design process for about a year.

I shared research responsibilities with the PM, and led co-creation, rapid prototyping, usability testing and validation.   Skills   Concept validation, user research, data dashboard design, co-creation, rapid prototyping, dogfooding   Collaborators   I worked closely with the PM at first, as I was the sole designer for the first several months. Later on, two designers joined the project on temporary rotations. By enlarge, I worked remotely from San Francisco, while the rest of the team was based in Denver.    Roadmapping   Choosing the Right Design Tools      At the onset, we had a lot of questions and not a lot of time. I was meeting with the PM everyday, and during our discussions we started capturing questions we were asking ourselves.    ...see moreexpand_more     We had big questions like, “Is the product viable?” and, “Should we base our product on an open source solution?” The roadmap helped us agree on priorities and map them out on a calendar, which helped us manage stakeholder expectations.     Our design roadmap made clear what we were doing and why.  Our roadmap calendar shows timelines.   Research   Understanding Our Place in the Ecosystem      When we kicked things off, our top concern was that we would need to displace entrenched products in order to be successful. With that in mind, we created a topic map and set up interviews with around 30 customer platform operators and Pivotal field engineers (who work onsite at our customers’ offices).    ...see moreexpand_more Research Goals Validation of an out-of-the-box GUI: Would customers even use our GUI? Our top concern was that we would have to displace entrenched competitors in order for our GUI to get traction. Understanding the reality of customer platform monitoring: How do customers currently monitor their platforms? What works well? What is painful?  The PM and I created an affinity map together in which we grouped findings into themes, such as “product-market fit” and “learning process”.  Key Findings    After a few weeks of user interviews and synthesis, a few themes surfaced to the top that we felt addressed our concerns:    Third-party tools are hard to learn: Third-party tools are sophisticated and take time to learn. This can be a burden for customers who are new to Cloud Foundry—several customers had tried to use third-party tools for platform monitoring, but gave up.

Data overload: There are thousands of metrics and customers don’t know what to monitor. Only a relatively small number of metrics are really needed to be effective, the trick is to know which ones. Platform monitoring dashboards are "snowflakes": In the long run, customer reporting needs are unique and operations teams will create dashboards that fit their needs.    “The last thing we want is the end user telling us about problems with our applications.” - Platform operator   Target Persona: New Operators    Our target users were platform operators who were new to monitoring. Despite that their teams tend to be small, with four to ten members, there can be more than a thousand developers using the platform once it is at scale.    Frustrations Data overload—There is too much data and it's difficult to make sense of Platform-monitoring software is difficult to learn Flying blind—Doesn't know about issues until there is an outage  Quotes “The last thing we want is the end user telling us about problems with our applications.” "Platform issues are almost always caused by humans, and it's almost always because someone was doing an update."   A Non-linear Journey    Our experience map captured that we would be part of a larger ecosystem: When there is a potential issue with the platform, our users go on a journey through a constellation of alerts, dashboards, and Slack conversations.       Our work would manifest as touchpoints along that journey—sometimes as UIs, sometimes not.     The journey map for HealthWatch was non-linear  Strategy   Giving Users a Bridge to Mastery      Around this time, we started thinking about how we might fit in the market: Advanced users will just use the API, but those new to the platform might benefit from an opinionated GUI that would be a bridge for them as they got up and running.    ...see moreexpand_more  Define KPIs: A lot of monitoring know-how was in the heads of our field and R&D engineers. The PM consolidated and documented this know-how in the form of KPIs. Provide an API: We knew that our customers would eventually create custom solutions, so we made our metrics available by API. Design a GUI: We used the KPIs as a basis for a UI that would be exemplar of how to visualize and organize metrics. This ended up being a two-way street as some of our designs defined KPIs.

Design Posture    As new users gain skill, they should graduate to creating their own dashboards using the API.      Collaboration    Making Feedback Continuous       Soon after we started rapid prototyping, we realized that we faced a seemingly insurmountable task: We needed to become experts in a complex domain in order to help users become experts in the domain.    ...see moreexpand_more Expert Advisory Panel    We reached out through our networks and found coworkers with deep knowledge of platform monitoring and fully embedded them in the design process.    A session with the expert advisory panel.    We made feedback a forcing function. This is how it worked: I committed to presenting to the panel whatever I was working on, in whatever state it was in, every Friday morning.        It worked well—In addition to getting continuous feedback, we built our own education into the process.       We faced a seemingly insurmountable task: We needed to become experts in a complex domain in order to help users become experts in the domain.   Co-creation    We had Cloud Foundry operators walk us through a time when they had to troubleshoot a problem. We then went to a whiteboard and co-designed the dashboard the operator wished they had.    Co-creation brought deep domain expertise to our understanding of the problems we were solving.     Here are a couple of interesting things we learned:    The question “When?” is paramount: “When did the system first start to have problems?” is one of the first things operators want to know. We made timestamps as precise as possible. Component IDs and timestamps are keys: Operators bounce between our app and others, such as log search tools. Component IDs and timestamps are the two things you need when jumping between systems. We add copy-to-clipboard buttons to everything we could. Weekly Engineering Reviews    Every Monday, I got the whole team together for 30 minutes and present whatever designs I was working on: low-fi sketches, user flows, wireframes, UI components, etc. I then got the engineering team to offer any thoughts or concerns they might have.    Cost vs. User Value    By putting our in-progress designs on the table for engineering every week, we got an early gut-check for complexity. This got the value/cost discussion going early and meant we could react to complexity with intention.      No-brainers    Low-cost, high-value features can go into the backlog.

Zone of Excess Compexity    Some features have user value, but are costly. Design should collaborate with engineering to lower costs.     Required or Differentiating    High-cost, high-value features are either must-be's (the product doesn't work without them), or market differentiators.      Rapid Prototyping   Rapid Prototyping Example: HTTP Requests      In our situation, it was almost impossible to thoroughly understand metrics and all their implications up front. We needed to just get ideas out of our heads and into the world quickly and start learning. Rapid prototyping means being wrong constantly until you gain confidence that you aren't.     Evolution of a metrics panel.    The design above on the left was our initial attempt at a router health design we presented to the advisory panel. It did not go over well. During our first feedback session, one panel member's first comment was, "I don't like anything about this."    Our first router panel design didn't go over well.   I probed the panel by asking, "What about this don't you like?" We quickly learned what we got wrong: We focused too much on router CPU and tried to force everything into a stoplight visualization. We didn't include overall traffic. So we went back to the drawing board. (Literally.)    Two more versions. We increased fidelity as our confidence in the design grew.   What we learned from the expert panel is that operators care first about overall traffic and whether it is trending up or down. If something is concerning, they then will want router details, such as latency and errors.      Layout and Hierarchy      For data dashboards, like in typography, meaning drives layout.      Through our iterative design process, we first understood the implications of metrics and how they naturally hung together with others. That drove layout decisions. For example, some indicators, like HTTP throughput, are key to interpreting others, and therefore are given prominence.      Interpreting a metric is often contingent on others. This informed layout decisions.  Logic Design    What Goes On Behind the Scenes for a Dashboard Stoplight       The simplicity of our CLI command stoplights belies the complex logic behind them. Cloud Foundry enables developers to push code onto the cloud easily, but behind this functionality are several systems all of which emit hundreds of metrics.    ...see moreexpand_more     Simplicity and Reliability        Rather than burdening users with interpreting these, HealthWatch provides an automated battery of tests.

The system automatically creates a minimal-footprint app, pushes it to the cloud, then tests it's health.          Everything is healthy.          Potential problem.          Users need to investigate.         Logic design    Each test (push, start, stop, etc.) are treated independentaly. For each suite of tests, individual stoplight error states are determined given the available information we have for each. For an isolated failure, the stoplight will be yellow. For sequnetial faillures (e.g., two push failure in a row), the stoplight will be red. Test that don't run are not counted. If all test do not run for a stoplight, stoplight will be gray.      Logic diagrams show test results (top rows) and how the results appear on the dashboard. This example shows how a self-correcting system can result in failed tests without displaying red stop lights.      Dogfooding    Making First Contact with Reality       HealthWatch would need to help operations teams navigate through unexpected disruptions, the nature of which can't be predicted. That meant we needed to test under real-world circumstances with real data.         HealthWatch in the wild. Our ops team in Dublin installed and used our alpha version and gave us weekly feedback.       Pivotal’s cloud operations team in Ireland agreed to be the first to use HealthWatch in a production environment. That team hosted a SaaS product called Pivotal Tracker, an agile project management tool which has several hundred-thousand active users.       Weekly Check-Ins       We conducted weekly check-in sessions. In these sessions, members of the operations teams walked us through scenarios where they used HealthWatch. We got a lot of usability feedback, but also validated that the app helped them get through unexpected problems, such as failed upgrades.       Card Sorting       Once the operations team had been using HealthWatch for several weeks, we gave operations team members workbooks in Google Draw that contained movable metrics panels. We then asked them to arrange the dashboard the way they would like it.       After that, we interviewed the operators to understand the thinking behind their layouts. This eventually led us to grouping metrics into three areas of impact.        After using the app for a few weeks, alpha testers arranged their ideal dashboard layout.     END-USER IMPACT     End users affected. Generally urgent.     DEVELOPER IMPACT     Not as urgent as end-user impact, but potentially important.

PLATFORM IMPACT     Underlying VMs and other platform infrastructure.         Feature Vetting       After the card sort, we had team members independently tell us what metrics they would remove from the main dashboard and why. This got us to declutter and move about 25 percent of the main dashboard to deep dive views.        Alpha testers voted metrics off the island.     Conclusion    HealthWatch's Impact       Measurable Outcomes       We hypothesized that five of seven potential customers would install our closed beta and give us three feedback sessions. This tested whether customers would be willing to "pay" for beta access and worked as a leading indicator of adoption. The first five signed up.       Customer Impact       In one of our beta feedback sessions, a customer kept repeating the phrase, "This is golden". A few weeks before, his team started hearing complaints from developers that they couldn't push their apps onto the platform.       What nobody knew at the time was that the platform had run out of memory. That resulted in days of frustration that would not have occurred if they had our product—the team would have been alerted to low memory long before running out.       U.S. Patent       About one year after our GA (generally available) release, I shared a U.S. patent with the PM and engineering lead.       Diagram from patent No. US20190188107A1 for “Health monitoring for cloud computing platforms”.       Impact on Pivotal's Design Practice       User-centered design was not widely practiced in Pivotal's R&D department when we started. After launch, I gave two lightning talks and wrote an essay on our process (which was published in PC World and translated into German).       Impact on Marketing and Sales       Dogfooding allowed for our sales and marketing teams to say, “We use this product ourselves.” I also heard from the field that the out-of-the-box UI played well in sales demos.     menu  chevron_left Case Studies HealthWatch Role and Team Roadmapping Research Strategy Collaboration Rapid prototyping Logic design Dog fooding Conclusion Knative Role and Team Strategy Design Strategy Story Mapping Competitive Analysis Ideation Rapid Prototyping Wireframing Alpha Release Conclusion Dev Console Role and Team Roadmapping Research Personas Workshop Prep Problem Prioritization Causal Mapping Solutioning Product Vision Storyboard Conclusion Obama How it works Role and Team Concepting Wireframing People Pivot Development Conclusion     © Copyright 2023 by Kevin Gates.    kgates@gmail.com LinkedIn  .

olio app.herokuapp.com obama?sn=1.  Kevin Gates - Obama   Kevin Gates       Kevin Gates Case Studies   Projects / Writing      Ask My AI    TL;DR: Project Atlas is an app that highlights the Obama campaign's main themes, all taylored to voter location. It launched on the president's twitter feed and reached his 42 million followers. To get to the finishline, I had to keep an all-volunteer tech team motivated while performing a hard pivot.      Getting Creative Under a Hard Deadline      A Vague Idea and a Looming Election      In June of 2012, I was given a big data set about President Obama's achievements, but not much more than a vague direction: make something voter-facing, and make it location-based.      The election was four months away and we had no idea where we would end up. This is what we came up with...     Project Atlas detects the user's location (in this case Orange County, Florida) and shows Obama's achievements, all taylored to be as local as possible.  How it works Geo-targeting Obama's Achievements   After exploring several concepts, pursuing a direction, pivoting mid-project, we came up with a solution and launched five weeks before election day. This is how it works:   STEP ONE   A voter gets a tweet from Obama inviting them to check out a map. They click it.       arrow_downward    STEP TWO   The app detects the voter's location. In this case they are in Orange County, Florida.       arrow_downward    STEP THREE   The app compiles a webpage about Obama's achievements, all tailored to Orange County, Florida.     Role and Team My Role   Based in the San Francisco Technology Field Office, I led the ideation, wireframing and ran weekly presentations to campaign headquarters. In addition, I led the dev team, wrangled data, coordinated the efforts of volunteers, and wrote a good portion of the JavaScript and CSS.   SKILLS   Concept exploration, geospatial analysis (PostGIS, QuantumGIS), wireframing, JavaScript, Google Maps API   COLLABORATORS   I worked with a director of product management in the Chicago headquarters. Two engineers volunteered 8 - 16 hours a week, a dozen volunteers helped wrangle data, and the visual design was polished by the branding team.

Conceptiing    Exploring the Data With Geospatial Tools       Research from campaign headquarters revealed that voters respond more favorably to Obama's achievements when they are made relevant to them. With that in mind, I set out to better understand the shape of the data and what might be done with it. My goal was to come up with ideas to pitch to our stakeholders that would resonate with the campaign's goals.             I set up geospatial tools (PostgreSQL, PostGIS and QuantumGIS) and started exploring the data. I considered a lot of possibilities: children gaining health care; renewable energy investments; tax savings, and many more.       Examples of maps I created to explore the campaign's data sets using PostgreSQL, PostGIS and QuantumGIS.     Wireframing    Refining a Direction       Our stakeholders liked the direction of a recovery-themed map I created, which used data about the 2009 stimulus bill. We got the go-ahead to move forward.       The first wireframes showed stimulus projects color-coded by category. These categories corresponded to the campaign's core policy areas: education, energy, health and infrastructure.       Using Google's mapping API, we could detect the users location, and just show data points from their area.         The first wireframes I created show the top five recovery projects per state. Projects are color-coded to correspond to campaign themes: education, energy, health and infrastructure. The maps were created using real data.         As the user zooms in, more and more projects are revealed. At each zoom level, the most relevant projects are highlighted in the inset at the left.      People    Inspiring Volunteers       With a green light from headquarters, momentum started building. We would need engineers and since most of the data was uncategorized, we would need volunteers to help categorize data.    ...see moreexpand_more    The question lingered: How could we motivate an all-volunteer team to hit hard deadlines? Here are a few approaches:    Assume intrinsic motivation: The engineers who volunteered were willing to go through an interview process, so they likely want to help the president get re-elected. Act like shipping product is inevitable: If it looks like we are going to ship, engineers will be more likely to help. Momentum then builds in a virtuous cycle. Be clear about scope: Break work down into doable chunks. Be sure volunteers know exactly what is expected of them and when things need to be done.

The question lingered: How could we motivate an all-volunteer team to hit hard deadlines?     Pivot    Adapting to Chaos       In July, things abruptly changed for the worse. Just as the recovery map concept started to take shape, the Romney campaign attacked the stimulus bill. The Obama campaign banned the mention of it. The project was stopped in its tracks.    ...see moreexpand_more A New Direction    Everything could have easily ended at this point. Rather than give up, I went back and re-evaluated the data sets we hadn't used, and came up with a new solution.       The new concept detected the user's location and displayed the president's accomplishments across all of the campaign's policy areas. The digital director at headquarters liked the new direction, and gave us the green light.      Campaign headquarters put the kibosh on our project. We were stopped in our tracks.       Early version of our new direction. This concept pulls in relevant data about health, education, transportation etc.     Development    Hitting a Tight Deadline       Due to time constraints, I stopped wire framing and worked directly with the prototype we had started. Our goal was to have a fully functional app with just enough opinion about the UI. The branding team in Chicago would take it from there.    Mobile-first    We wanted to make sure the app would work well on mobile. As we wrote the code and defined the UI, we tested constantly on several phones and tablets.        Using a responsive UI framework, I was able to simultaneously design for tablets and phones, helping us keep to a very tight timeline.        An early fully-functioning version.      The final version of the app after the branding team's work.    Conclusion   Impact: Reaching Millions of Voters      The final weeks of the campaign were total chaos. Despite this, we managed to launch the morning of October 1st. Later that day, the campaign sent the URL out on Obama's Twitter account, where it reached the president's 42 million followers.

The tweet that went out October 1, 2012    menu  chevron_left Case Studies HealthWatch Role and Team Roadmapping Research Strategy Collaboration Rapid prototyping Logic design Dog fooding Conclusion Knative Role and Team Strategy Design Strategy Story Mapping Competitive Analysis Ideation Rapid Prototyping Wireframing Alpha Release Conclusion Dev Console Role and Team Roadmapping Research Personas Workshop Prep Problem Prioritization Causal Mapping Solutioning Product Vision Storyboard Conclusion Obama How it works Role and Team Concepting Wireframing People Pivot Development Conclusion     © Copyright 2023 by Kevin Gates.    kgates@gmail.com LinkedIn  .

olio app.herokuapp.com devconsole.  Kevin Gates - Developer Console   Kevin Gates        Kevin Gates Case Studies   Projects / Writing      Ask My AI     TL;DR: For enterprise developers, getting access to software-building tools on cloud platforms is rife with bottlenecks and red tape. We reimagined a better experience by anchoring product ideas around a nuanced understanding of customer problems. Our design was the product of transdisciplinary ideation, which was energizing and fruitful.       A Human-Centered Approach to Enterprise Problems       A Big Ask       This project started as a challenge posed by Pivotal's director of product for its London R&D org. She wanted me to do two things:    Discover the top problems engineers in large enterprises have in getting the tools they need to build software. Organize a product team anchored around those problems and come up with solutions.     It was a big ask, but also a big opportunity.      Role and Team My Role   As design lead, I drove research and synthesis, facilitated a week-long ideation workshop and design sprint, and created a product vision storyboard.   SKILLS   User research, workshop facilitation, collaborative ideation, storyboarding   COLLABORATORS   For the research, I paired with an engineer (and aspiring designer) on a daily basis. After that, I facilitated design workshops which included an R&D engineering team, a PM, and a new-hire designer. I also had weekly one-on-ones with the director of product.     Roadmapping    A Collaborative Product Roadmap       We prioritized team work by first understanding the problems we faced. I facilitated a workshop in which engineers, PMs and design formed open questions around our central theme of developer tool access. We then prioritized problems and brainstormed activities.    ...see moreexpand_more       My roadmapping workshops start with the whole team collectively agreeing on top problems, then brainstorming activities.          Each product activity has a description, outcome, output and timeframe. (This doc is from a more recent project.)       Research Discovering the Reality of the Enterprise Developer Experience     In our roadmapping workshop, the consensus was that we needed to learn more about the app developer experience. We interviewed around 30 customers, non-customers, and members of our consulting business who work on-site.    ...see moreexpand_more    The interviews were split between app devs and platform operators.

We wanted to find out things like how developers decide what tools they need and what they find frustrating.         My roadmapping workshops start with the whole team collectively agreeing on top problems, then brainstorming activities.          Each product activity has a description, outcome, output and timeframe. (This doc is from a more recent project.)         Making Synthesis a Team Effort       We rotated teammates onto the synthesis process so they could see for themselves how insights emerge from data. There are key benefits to doing this:    Transparency: Having teammates rotate onto design activities lets them see how we discover patterns. That builds credibility, which then builds trust in the data and paves the way for evidence-based decision making. Alternate perspectives: We were doing research in a very technical domain. Engineers were in a good position to help us find patterns we might not have found ourselves.     Once our affinity map started taking shape, we asked for volunteers to help. In this picture, the engineer I collaborated with described affinity mapping to another engineer as “optimization through evolution”.       Our affinity map was a work horse: it worked as a reference that informed many other artifacts and activities.     Personas    Understanding Misaligned Priorities       At the heart of everything we learned was a fundamental conflict: While app developers want a wide variety of tools and the latest, hot technology, platform operators want to offer a small, manageable set of tools.    ...see moreexpand_more App Developer       App developers build apps and other types of software. They can have diverse roles ranging from data science to mobile app development. Large organizations can employ thousands of developers.       Character-istics 97 to 99 percent of our customers’ developers are early in their careers. Most are focused on writing code and are not comfortable working with cloud infrastructure.  Key driver App devs want access to a wide variety of tools and the latest hot tech.  Underlying motovators Role-specific needs: Developers come in many forms. A data scientist requires a very different set of tools than a mobile app developer. Task-specific needs: Engineers sometimes start with specific needs related to the task at hand. For example, an aerospace engineering team creating flight control tests might need a time-series database with specified level of latency. Resume value: Developers often want the latest hot tech. This is partly due to resume value, which can determine their viability in the job market.     Having the latest hot tech on a resume can determine a developer's viability in the job market.

Platform Operator       Platform operators maintain the cloud platform. They are responsible for providing tools for developers, keeping the platform up-to-date, and troubleshooting when something goes wrong.      Character-istics Even in large organizations, operator teams tend to be small, with 10 - 20 people. Their small team sizes contrast with the fact that their platforms can be used by hundreds, even thousands of developers.  Key driver Operators want to offer a small, manageable set of tools that cover a wide surface area.  Underlying motovators Risk aversion: Cloud platforms are extremely complex, but tend to be stable as long as nothing changes. Introducing new tools means introducing change, and change brings risk of something going wrong. Developing expertise: If an operations team offers a new technology on their platform, they are expected to be experts at that technology. When something goes wrong, the operations teams are expected to fix it. Not enough demand: Operations teams are inherently small and they tend to be unwilling to support tools for which there is little demand.  Our Primary Personas Are Fundamentally Misaligned    At the heart of everything was a fundamental conflict: While app developers want a wide variety of tools and the latest, hot technology, platform operators want to offer a small, manageable set of tools.    Seeing the Journey as a Series of Conflicts Between Personas    We visualized the misalignment between personas as a cartoon argument. We then mapped the argument onto a skeleton version of the journey. That conveyed where on the journey misalignment can lead to bottle necks and frustration.          Appdev wants cool tech.vs.Operator wants to serve the larger organization.         App dev wants help learning a new tool.vs.Operator doesn't have the time to help individuals.         Areas of conflict were represented as red dots on the journey.     Dev Tool Access Patterns    One way or another, developers tend to get the tools they need, often through off-platform sources. Some of these, such as dedicated service teams, are sanctioned, while others, such as developers going "DIY", are not, which can lead to security and compliance risk.       Diagrams from our research findings deck that depict the various ways developers get the tools they need from a variety of sources.     Workshop Prep    Feedback as a Forcing Function       Before ideation week began, I set up sources of feedback and embedded them into the whole process. Getting continuous feedback would help keep us sane as we navigated a vast problem space.

PANEL OF ADVISORY EXPERTS     I recruited a panel that included people from related product teams, field engineers and marketing from Europe and North America. I set up two sessions with them on Wednesday and Friday, and presented our progress. The panel brought relevant and diverse perspectives to the table.     FIELD ENGINEERS     Several field engineers volunteered their time. We included them in activities and asked them to evaluate early ideas. Field engineers are great because they often work on-site for months at a time and can bring the customer perspective to the table.     OPEN DOORS     I invited the whole Pivotal office to stop by any time. When anyone showed up, one of us would take the lead and explain our current thinking, and how we got there. Random visits made sure we could clearly articulate everything we were doing at any time.        Getting continuous feedback would help keep us sane as we navigated a vast problem space.     Problem Prioritization    Determining Where We Can Have the Most Impact       With many customer problems at hand, we needed to decide which ones to tackle. We used three techniques:    Surveying our field engineers to assess where we can impact customer needs. Determining where we, as a product team, can move the needle. Assessing any engineering risks.   Wheelhouse check     We sorted the top problems into three columns: Solvable by us, solvable through collaboration with another team, not in our wheelhouse.      Engineering Risk Assessment     Engineers offered an early assessment of engineering risk. Even at this early stage, we felt we could get a sense of complexity early, and take than into consideration.     Top Customer Problems    By noon the first day, we had honed in on a handful of customer problems that were both high-impact (according to our field team survey) and problems for which an engineering R&D team could move the needle.        After our vetting process, a handful of customer problems rose to the top.     Causal Mapping    Seeing the Big Picture        Our causal map starts with a customer problem at the left (there is a lot more demand for developer tools than supply). The yellow nodes are root causes and the blue nodes are possible solutions.    Root Cause Analysis    With help from the field, we took our top problems and explored root causes by asking ourselves "why?" as many times as possible. This would help us:    See relationships between problems and have a more holistic perspective.

Identify big pain points that lead to a lot of down-stream problems. Expanded the field of potential problems we could solve.     Detail showing tiers of causes for one of our top customer problems   Down-Stream Effects     We explored how problems often cascade into dysfunction, higher costs and frustration by asking ourselves "so what?" These down-stream effects link customer problems to value props.       This diagram links a "how might we?" solution (top) to value (at the bottom).     Solutioning    How Might We?       On Tuesday, after dot voting on root causes, we took the top problems and explored solution ideas with a “How might we?” exercise. We grouped ideas by theme, then discussed and dot-voted.         Morning review of the previous day's work (photo credit: Miguel Luna)    Advisory Panel Feedback    On Wednesday we presented our top initial directions to the advisory panel and took notes. Two solution themes rose to the top. Both were under the question, “How might we allow more [tools] to be available?”.        High-level solution directions that rose to the top.    How Might We?... Round Two    We had a clear direction from the advisory panel, but our concepts were very high-level, and we needed to drill into them them further. We did this by taking each concept (e.g., "Tiered SLAs") and expanding through more "how might we?" exercises.        Diagram showing chains of solutions that originate from “How might we allow more tools to be available?”.     Product Vision    Tying the Story Together    Scenario writing    On Thursday, we took our top ideas and wrote scenarios describing a future in which our customers are enjoying the value of our not-yet-existent products. We were envisioning presenting a storyboard to the advisory panel on Friday and wanted our ideas to be approachable and expressive.        Scenario writing session      Our scenario writing didn't work out as planned. We would need to improvise.   A Misfire and Redress    “What is the point of scenario writing? Have we learned anything?”, asked an engineer on Friday morning. I probed the room. “What do we think?”, I asked.       In short, our solution ideas were not solid enough technically for our engineers. For a lot of types of projects, safe assumptions can be made about the tech. That was not the case for us.       We went back to the whiteboard and discussed.

The solution was to group ideas into product concepts. Those allowed the engineers to envision the "how" to a level that was acceptable.   Idea Boards and Advisory Panel Feedback, Round Two    We still needed something to present to the advisory panel. We came up with idea boards. Idea boards describe product concepts, and tie together outcomes, evidence and personas.       We created an idea board for each of our top concepts and presented to the advisory panel for feedback.       Idea board example    INDEPENDENT SERVICE OFFERINGS     Outcome: Operators can delegate the curation and management of services to non-operators, effectively increasing the offerings.      DIFFERENT LEVELS OF SUPPORT     Outcome: Developers can provision a wider variety of tools that are not mission-critical (such as databases with lower uptime) and are managed by non-operators.      AUTOMATED TOOL INTEGRATION TESTS     Outcome: Third-party tool providers have increased confidence in an integration before releasing it. Operators have increased confidence in an integration before managing it.       Storyboard    Externalizing the Product Vision       After ideation week, I worked with marketing and an illustrator to bring the prodcut vision to life.        Conclusion    Design Impact       Design Sprint       Our ideation session was the foundation for a compact, two-day design sprint. (I plan to include the sprint as a separate case study.)       Research Impact       Our research deck got a lot of attention. I presented it several times as part of our integration process. At several points there were dozens of people in the Google doc, including vice presidents. One PM told me she had been referring to the deck on a regular basis. In addition, I later used the research findings to inform two other related projects.       Design Practice Impact       I documented the design process and presented it internally and externally, including two times for the U.K. Government Digital Services.       Learnings    If something is not working, nip it in the bud and pivot: Our initial scenario-based presentation didn't gel. We took the outcome we were after and came up with a solution that worked. High-trust between team members enables efficiency: High trust enables critical feedback, which was necessary for us to recover from mistakes. Transdisciplinary ideation is powerful: We gained insights that only came about because designers, engineers and field engineers were working together on the same problem. Marketing team members on our advisory panel had feedback that the rest of us would not have thought of.

Storyboards are great tools for conveying vision: I got feedback that people had no idea of what we were up to until they saw the storyboard. In large organizations, I've found people will hear about your project, assume worst-case scenario, and imagine conflict that doesn't exist. A good vision storyboard can dispell mistrust and open doors for collaboration.  menu  chevron_left Case Studies HealthWatch Role and Team Roadmapping Research Strategy Collaboration Rapid prototyping Logic design Dog fooding Conclusion Knative Role and Team Strategy Design Strategy Story Mapping Competitive Analysis Ideation Rapid Prototyping Wireframing Alpha Release Conclusion Dev Console Role and Team Roadmapping Research Personas Workshop Prep Problem Prioritization Causal Mapping Solutioning Product Vision Storyboard Conclusion Obama How it works Role and Team Concepting Wireframing People Pivot Development Conclusion     © Copyright 2023 by Kevin Gates.    kgates@gmail.com LinkedIn  .

olio app.herokuapp.com knative?sn=1.  Kevin Gates - Knative UI   Kevin Gates       Kevin Gates Case Studies   Projects / Writing      Ask My AI    TL;DR: KNative, a popular open-source tool, automatically frees up unused memory in the cloud, creating big cost savings for IT departments. We created a UX for it that lowers barriers to adoption, enabling those savings to scale. Our collaborative approach to design produced market-differentiating features.    	  Humanizing Open Source 	  	  Starting From the Ground Up 	  	  When a product manager and I kicked off this project, our initial idea was to perform a heuristic evaluation of KNative’s open-source GUI, make a few recommendations, and that would be the end of it. 	  	  Instead, what we found was a UI that had been given little attention. We couldn’t fix it half way—it needed a redesign from the ground up. 	   The Problem 	  Data Model as the UI 	  	  Overall, the open-source UI looked like a reflection of a data model, and did not have well-thought-out flows, hierarchy, or layout. Below are a few examples of problems we found: 	   Data Tables 	   The main interface consisted of four tables which seemed like tables you might find in a database (configurations, status messages, routes, revisions). 	    Disjointed Layout 	   Information was not laid out logically. For example, system status information relevant to the table in the lower left appeared on the upper right. 	    Poor UX Flow 	   The only actions available were a bit of clickable text on the upper left that said "configuration", which led to an editable config file, and a delete button on the upper right. 	     Role and Team My Role 	  Over a period of six weeks, I drove the overall approach to design as the design lead. I worked closely with a PM in planning workshops, conducting user interviews, and rapid prototyping. 	  SKILLS 	  Design strategy, competitive analysis, collaborative ideation, rapid prototyping, usability testing 	  COLLABORATORS 	  On a daily basis, I worked with a product manager and engineering lead. Two visual designers rotated onto the project towards the end. 	   Strategy 	  Assessing Risk in the World of Open Source 	   	   We were building on open-source technology that had already survived contact with reality. That meant the functionality had already been proven useful, but we still needed to figure out what value-add we could bring.

...see moreexpand_more  	   Demand 	   	   Demand for serverless technology is well-established and growing. According to the 2020 Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF) survey: 	   30% of organizations polled have production apps running on serverless and another 35% are considering it. The CNCF "fully expect growth to continue". 82% of organizations who are using serverless are using hosted solutions.  	   Is your organization using serverless? 	     	   How is your organization using serverless technology? 	     	   VALUE 	   	   KNative has been validated in the marketplace and demonstrably solves well-known IT problems: 	   Resource efficiency: Serverless automatically frees up unused memory and CPU allocations in the cloud (“scales to zero”). Emergency recovery: Serverless automatically creates application backups. Traffic splitting: Serverless makes it easy to split traffic between versions for A/B testing and gradual rollouts.   Design Strategy Targeting Users By Skill Level 	   Near the start of the design process, I like the team to imagine it's two years after launch and the product is very successful. I then ask, "Who is the average user? What are they like?" 	   ...see moreexpand_more 	  From previous research, we knew a few things relevant to our project: 	  Most large-scale enterprises, 97 to 99% of developers are early-career and don’t have skills in managing and configuring cloud-based infrastructure. Many developers at large organizations are contractors (up to 50%) who often don't have direct access to the cloud platform and rely on GUIs. Some customers have elite engineering teams who use commandline tools and automate processes. 	  Our design strategy took this into account: We assumed most developers will be early-career and new to serverless, but will gain skill over time. 	    	  This matrix shows how we targeted users by skill level, and who by definition should be the largest user cohort. 	    Story Mapping Laying Out a Goal-Directed Narrative 	   The PM had created story maps by analyzing the open-source functionality, which did a good job at outlining features and aligning them with outcomes. 	   ...see moreexpand_more 	  The problem is they didn't capture the narrative of the experience. User experiences are a series of activities that take place in the physical world in service of people achieving goals, which are often interconnected in logical ways.

Story Map Workshop 	  	  I facilitated a workshop in which we deconstructed the story maps and created a narrative in the form of what I call a proto-journey map. 	    	  Our proto-journey map layed out a flow centered on user goals. 	   	  First and foremost, we wanted to have a foundation for ideation sessions in which we rethink the journey. In addition, we wanted to: 	  Create a narrative which allow us to see the big picture and how tasks are connected and in service of the user reaching goals. Have a way to fold in and organize other materials such as research findings and competitive analysis.  	  Anatomy of an experience map. Stories were deconstructed and arranged a linear narrative with tasks, goals and milestones. 	    Competitive Analysis Understanding the Competition 	   We took screenshots of our competitor's UIs and pasted them along our journey map, aligning them to user tasks. Our goal was to make sure we had parity with Google, IBM and the OS version, and to find inspiration in things they do well. 	   ...see moreexpand_more   	  Screenshots of our competitor's UIs were aligned with user goals (left). Miro's zoom feature allowed us to zoom into screenshots and see details. 	   COMPETITOR STRENGTHS We liked Google’s stepper approach to creating and updating serverless apps and contextual help. RedHat had advanced features we liked, such as allowing more experienced users to paste configurations into the UI rather than fill out a form.  COMPETITOR WEAKNESSES Most UIs reflected data structures, not user mental models. Elements in UI not logically grouped together. Poor / confusing wayfinding. System status messages incoherent. Lack of guided step-by-step flows or beginners (“Paste YAML here”).    Ideation Sketching a Differentiating UX 	   Now that we understood something about our competitors and we had a model of the user journey, we set up an ideation workshop (a.k.a., design studio) in which everyone (engineers, field engineers, design and PM) would sketch concepts. The goal was to get diverse perspectives to seed our creative process. 	   ...see moreexpand_more  	  Market Differentiators 	  	  We dot-voted sketches and discussed what we liked. A few solved key user problems in creative ways, and had the potential to differentiate us from competitors.

Intuitive Layout 	   Several people came up with similar designs that merged information from multiple sources into intuitive tables. Many had a visual representation of status. 	    Perfomance Analysis 	   Serverless technology will automatically free up system resources when an app has no traffic. When traffic returns, starting up cold can be slow. Exercising the scaling functionality, then showing how long cold starts take would enable developers to optimize for more responsiveness. 	    Change Log 	   Changes to apps (revisions) are described in YAML. This feature would create a diff between revisions, allowing developers to see exactly what changed. This could be an interesting place to start debugging. 	     Rapid Prototyping Iterative Testing and Evaluation 	   UX design for cloud computing requires navigating a lot of complexity. I've found an iterative approach with constant feedback is the best way to work through it. 	   ...see moreexpand_more  	   Setting Up User Feedback 	   	   We set feedback from many types of users: experts, novices, field engineers, internal engineers. For each of these, we had two types of learning goals: 	   Evaluative (a.k.a., usability): Can the user complete given tasks? What about developers with no experience with serverless, can they understand everything? Generative: When do developers use GUIs versus command lines? Why do they use them? What would they change in our UI, if anything? 	   Prototyping and Testing 	   	   With the PM and I largely aligned on our approach, I created our first prototype based on our key user scenarios. 	     	  Our usability tests had three primary user scenarios: Create, update and route traffic. We synthesized results directly on the wire frames to maintain context. 	   	  Key Findings 	  Big frustrations were hidden in the details: Our first versions did not have default settings for memory allocations. The problem is that when the user is filling this field out, their app is likely in its early stages, and they would have no idea how much memory they would need at launch. This was a required field, so when you put everything together, we were forcing the user to answer a question they could not answer. Novice users were confused: A big part of the confusion was caused by a few terms associated with serverless technology that were overloaded (in particular, "service" and "traffic"). One tester thought he was configuring a database for the first 10 minutes of the interview.

GUIs can provide value for all users: GUIs can provide value for all users: GUIs can provide value for new users, early-career developers, seasoned veterans, and even expert users. This reinforced our initial assumptions.   Wireframing Aligning Competing Forces 	   After several iterations and feedback sessions, our wireframes started to feel more and more solid. There were several forces aligning in our designs: 	   ...see moreexpand_more  Business strategy: Lower barriers to adoption Design strategy: Target users by skill level Harmony: Stay true to KNative conventions User experience: Use well-known UX patterns 	   Sometimes these forces were complementary; sometimes they were competing. Below are examples of how they manifest themselves after many iterations and feedback sessions: 	     	   Contextual help supported all of our objectives. 	    	   Keeping open-sources terminology is important, especially when users go between the UI and command line. 	     	   We merged version information into a single table and included visual icons to represent status. 	    	   Our UI shows diffs between versions. The UI uses source control conventions well-known to developers. 	     Alpha Release A UX to Lower Barriers to Adoption and Enable Scale 	  Key Value Props 	  Users don't have to think about infrastructure: We aimed to allow users with no knowledge of Kubernetes or KNative to easily access their benefits. Features for all skill levels: We designed for early-career developers, but also included features that allow for more efficient workflows for our advanced users. Cost savings at scale: KNative manages memory allocation automatically which results in big cost savings. Making KNative easily accessible to developers allows those savings to scale.   	   Early design for our alpha release 	    	   Early design for our alpha release 	     Conclusion 	  Impact 	  	  Initial Feedback From the Field 	  	  As of this writing, the project was going into an internal alpha release. Initial feedback from our field team was very positive. “When a developer doesn’t know much about a new technology, just clicking a box is powerful.” was a response from a field engineer in EMEA. 	  	  Differentiating Features 	  	  Our design studio produced two features that our competitors do not have: change logs and scaling analysis. These features address the top two pain points for serverless technology: debugging and cold starts (according to the Serverless Community Survey).

Impact on the Design Practice 	  	  Our "target skill level" matrix went over well with other design teams, and was adopted for other projects. 	  	  Learnings 	  Opinionated APIs can be challenging: We designed a UI on top of two very opinionated APIs (KNative and Kubernetes) and those opinions were often at odds with good UX principles. Putting competitive analysis in the journey map worked: We found ourselves referring back to the analysis often, and I got feedback that others were doing the same.  menu  chevron_left Case Studies HealthWatch Role and Team Roadmapping Research Strategy Collaboration Rapid prototyping Logic design Dog fooding Conclusion Knative Role and Team Strategy Design Strategy Story Mapping Competitive Analysis Ideation Rapid Prototyping Wireframing Alpha Release Conclusion Dev Console Role and Team Roadmapping Research Personas Workshop Prep Problem Prioritization Causal Mapping Solutioning Product Vision Storyboard Conclusion Obama How it works Role and Team Concepting Wireframing People Pivot Development Conclusion     © Copyright 2023 by Kevin Gates.    kgates@gmail.com LinkedIn  .

Kevin Gates - Rx Flow   Kevin Gates       Kevin Gates Case Studies   Projects / Writing      Ask My AI       Traversing Patient Journeys   I created this prototype over the course of a few days when I was a data vizualization engineer at Practice Fusion. The head of the medical informatics ask me to experiment with how pharma analysts might explore patient journeys for a diabetes drug called Invokana.     The goal was to have a way for users to see a logitudinal view of what drug regimines Invokana patients have been prescribed, and where else in the jouney the drug might be benficial to them.   Starting with the big picture   The initial state enables users to explore patient journeys starting with all patients prescribed Invokana. They then work backwards, opening branches. Each branch leads to more patients, which are grouped by drug regimines.   Pharma analysts can explore patient cohort journeys of drug prescriptions. Selecting patient groups   Once the user finds a group of patient cohorts they are interested in, they can select them and see a detailed dashboard.     At the time I created this, I had read a paper by Jeffrey Heer and George Robertson, Animated Transitions in Statistical Data Graphics, and was inspired their findings around object tracking. In my implementation, users find the patient groups they want to inspect, select them, and the colored circles representing patient groups move to a new context.   Selecting a group of patient groups transitions to a detailed dashboard Exploring Cohort Details   The top columns are key patient attributes, the rows are patient cohorts grouped by drug regimine. Columns can be sorted. This is key: One of the rows is called "All Diabetics", a benchmark against which the others can be compared. Invokana patients should be younger and sicker than average, which shows up in the data.     Thoughts on the Prototype     If I were to explore this concept further, I thought it might be interesting to allow users to choose a starting point and an end point. In other words, users could start with a diagnosis of high blood pressure and end with Invokana. The UI might somehow show all paths between these points and give user to ability to filter and explore until they want a detailed dashboard.

Anyway, go ahead and try the prototype yourself!   menu  chevron_left Case Studies HealthWatch Role and Team Roadmapping Research Strategy Collaboration Rapid prototyping Logic design Dog fooding Conclusion Knative Role and Team Strategy Design Strategy Story Mapping Competitive Analysis Ideation Rapid Prototyping Wireframing Alpha Release Conclusion Dev Console Role and Team Roadmapping Research Personas Workshop Prep Problem Prioritization Causal Mapping Solutioning Product Vision Storyboard Conclusion Obama How it works Role and Team Concepting Wireframing People Pivot Development Conclusion     © Copyright 2023 by Kevin Gates.    kgates@gmail.com LinkedIn  .

olio app.herokuapp.com weather.  Kevin Gates - Weather   Kevin Gates       Kevin Gates Case Studies   Projects / Writing      Ask My AI        Exploration: How Might Users Interact With a Large Data Set On a Phone?   Have you ever flown to a new city—maybe Chicago or Stolkholm— and wondered how cold it might be when you need to walk to work in the morning? Forecasts are one thing, but they are not reliable, especially if they are more than a few days in the future. How might you be able to know what locals in Chicago or Stolkholm know intuitively?       Weather Forecasts with Historical Context      Say a user wants to know the temperature in New York City when they plan to walk to work—8 AM on November 4th. Imagine they could see what the weather tends to be like at that time based on 40 years of historical data.    Users can view historic weather data.     To do this, 8 AM temperatures for New York City on November 1st through November 7th would be collected (280 measurements), then percentiles would be calculated. The user could then see historics by dragging down on the chart.        This effectively answers two key questions forecasts do not: 1) How cold does it tend to be in Chicago at 8 am in early November?, and 2) How cold can it get?       Appendix: Applying Design Principles      As a designer, I've always liked the idea of getting designs translated into code quickly. React's concept of components and state made certain design principles relatively easy to implement, such as allowing users to recover from mistakes (a.k.a., "undo"). I also liked designing directly in code using FlexBox / CSS and skipping superfluous visual design steps. Below are a couple examples. olio app.herokuapp.com devconsole?sn=1.  Kevin Gates - Developer Console   Kevin Gates        Kevin Gates Case Studies   Projects / Writing      Ask My AI     TL;DR: For enterprise developers, getting access to software-building tools on cloud platforms is rife with bottlenecks and red tape. We reimagined a better experience by anchoring product ideas around a nuanced understanding of customer problems. Our design was the product of transdisciplinary ideation, which was energizing and fruitful.       A Human-Centered Approach to Enterprise Problems       A Big Ask       This project started as a challenge posed by Pivotal's director of product for its London R&D org. She wanted me to do two things:    Discover the top problems engineers in large enterprises have in getting the tools they need to build software. Organize a product team anchored around those problems and come up with solutions.     It was a big ask, but also a big opportunity.      Role and Team My Role   As design lead, I drove research and synthesis, facilitated a week-long ideation workshop and design sprint, and created a product vision storyboard.   SKILLS   User research, workshop facilitation, collaborative ideation, storyboarding   COLLABORATORS   For the research, I paired with an engineer (and aspiring designer) on a daily basis. After that, I facilitated design workshops which included an R&D engineering team, a PM, and a new-hire designer. I also had weekly one-on-ones with the director of product.     Roadmapping    A Collaborative Product Roadmap       We prioritized team work by first understanding the problems we faced. I facilitated a workshop in which engineers, PMs and design formed open questions around our central theme of developer tool access. We then prioritized problems and brainstormed activities.    ...see moreexpand_more       My roadmapping workshops start with the whole team collectively agreeing on top problems, then brainstorming activities.          Each product activity has a description, outcome, output and timeframe. (This doc is from a more recent project.)       Research Discovering the Reality of the Enterprise Developer Experience     In our roadmapping workshop, the consensus was that we needed to learn more about the app developer experience. We interviewed around 30 customers, non-customers, and members of our consulting business who work on-site.    ...see moreexpand_more    The interviews were split between app devs and platform operators.

We wanted to find out things like how developers decide what tools they need and what they find frustrating.         My roadmapping workshops start with the whole team collectively agreeing on top problems, then brainstorming activities.          Each product activity has a description, outcome, output and timeframe. (This doc is from a more recent project.)         Making Synthesis a Team Effort       We rotated teammates onto the synthesis process so they could see for themselves how insights emerge from data. There are key benefits to doing this:    Transparency: Having teammates rotate onto design activities lets them see how we discover patterns. That builds credibility, which then builds trust in the data and paves the way for evidence-based decision making. Alternate perspectives: We were doing research in a very technical domain. Engineers were in a good position to help us find patterns we might not have found ourselves.     Once our affinity map started taking shape, we asked for volunteers to help. In this picture, the engineer I collaborated with described affinity mapping to another engineer as “optimization through evolution”.       Our affinity map was a work horse: it worked as a reference that informed many other artifacts and activities.     Personas    Understanding Misaligned Priorities       At the heart of everything we learned was a fundamental conflict: While app developers want a wide variety of tools and the latest, hot technology, platform operators want to offer a small, manageable set of tools.    ...see moreexpand_more App Developer       App developers build apps and other types of software. They can have diverse roles ranging from data science to mobile app development. Large organizations can employ thousands of developers.       Character-istics 97 to 99 percent of our customers’ developers are early in their careers. Most are focused on writing code and are not comfortable working with cloud infrastructure.  Key driver App devs want access to a wide variety of tools and the latest hot tech.  Underlying motovators Role-specific needs: Developers come in many forms. A data scientist requires a very different set of tools than a mobile app developer. Task-specific needs: Engineers sometimes start with specific needs related to the task at hand. For example, an aerospace engineering team creating flight control tests might need a time-series database with specified level of latency. Resume value: Developers often want the latest hot tech. This is partly due to resume value, which can determine their viability in the job market.     Having the latest hot tech on a resume can determine a developer's viability in the job market.

Platform Operator       Platform operators maintain the cloud platform. They are responsible for providing tools for developers, keeping the platform up-to-date, and troubleshooting when something goes wrong.      Character-istics Even in large organizations, operator teams tend to be small, with 10 - 20 people. Their small team sizes contrast with the fact that their platforms can be used by hundreds, even thousands of developers.  Key driver Operators want to offer a small, manageable set of tools that cover a wide surface area.  Underlying motovators Risk aversion: Cloud platforms are extremely complex, but tend to be stable as long as nothing changes. Introducing new tools means introducing change, and change brings risk of something going wrong. Developing expertise: If an operations team offers a new technology on their platform, they are expected to be experts at that technology. When something goes wrong, the operations teams are expected to fix it. Not enough demand: Operations teams are inherently small and they tend to be unwilling to support tools for which there is little demand.  Our Primary Personas Are Fundamentally Misaligned    At the heart of everything was a fundamental conflict: While app developers want a wide variety of tools and the latest, hot technology, platform operators want to offer a small, manageable set of tools.    Seeing the Journey as a Series of Conflicts Between Personas    We visualized the misalignment between personas as a cartoon argument. We then mapped the argument onto a skeleton version of the journey. That conveyed where on the journey misalignment can lead to bottle necks and frustration.          Appdev wants cool tech.vs.Operator wants to serve the larger organization.         App dev wants help learning a new tool.vs.Operator doesn't have the time to help individuals.         Areas of conflict were represented as red dots on the journey.     Dev Tool Access Patterns    One way or another, developers tend to get the tools they need, often through off-platform sources. Some of these, such as dedicated service teams, are sanctioned, while others, such as developers going "DIY", are not, which can lead to security and compliance risk.       Diagrams from our research findings deck that depict the various ways developers get the tools they need from a variety of sources.     Workshop Prep    Feedback as a Forcing Function       Before ideation week began, I set up sources of feedback and embedded them into the whole process. Getting continuous feedback would help keep us sane as we navigated a vast problem space.

PANEL OF ADVISORY EXPERTS     I recruited a panel that included people from related product teams, field engineers and marketing from Europe and North America. I set up two sessions with them on Wednesday and Friday, and presented our progress. The panel brought relevant and diverse perspectives to the table.     FIELD ENGINEERS     Several field engineers volunteered their time. We included them in activities and asked them to evaluate early ideas. Field engineers are great because they often work on-site for months at a time and can bring the customer perspective to the table.     OPEN DOORS     I invited the whole Pivotal office to stop by any time. When anyone showed up, one of us would take the lead and explain our current thinking, and how we got there. Random visits made sure we could clearly articulate everything we were doing at any time.        Getting continuous feedback would help keep us sane as we navigated a vast problem space.     Problem Prioritization    Determining Where We Can Have the Most Impact       With many customer problems at hand, we needed to decide which ones to tackle. We used three techniques:    Surveying our field engineers to assess where we can impact customer needs. Determining where we, as a product team, can move the needle. Assessing any engineering risks.   Wheelhouse check     We sorted the top problems into three columns: Solvable by us, solvable through collaboration with another team, not in our wheelhouse.      Engineering Risk Assessment     Engineers offered an early assessment of engineering risk. Even at this early stage, we felt we could get a sense of complexity early, and take than into consideration.     Top Customer Problems    By noon the first day, we had honed in on a handful of customer problems that were both high-impact (according to our field team survey) and problems for which an engineering R&D team could move the needle.        After our vetting process, a handful of customer problems rose to the top.     Causal Mapping    Seeing the Big Picture        Our causal map starts with a customer problem at the left (there is a lot more demand for developer tools than supply). The yellow nodes are root causes and the blue nodes are possible solutions.    Root Cause Analysis    With help from the field, we took our top problems and explored root causes by asking ourselves "why?" as many times as possible. This would help us:    See relationships between problems and have a more holistic perspective.

Identify big pain points that lead to a lot of down-stream problems. Expanded the field of potential problems we could solve.     Detail showing tiers of causes for one of our top customer problems   Down-Stream Effects     We explored how problems often cascade into dysfunction, higher costs and frustration by asking ourselves "so what?" These down-stream effects link customer problems to value props.       This diagram links a "how might we?" solution (top) to value (at the bottom).     Solutioning    How Might We?       On Tuesday, after dot voting on root causes, we took the top problems and explored solution ideas with a “How might we?” exercise. We grouped ideas by theme, then discussed and dot-voted.         Morning review of the previous day's work (photo credit: Miguel Luna)    Advisory Panel Feedback    On Wednesday we presented our top initial directions to the advisory panel and took notes. Two solution themes rose to the top. Both were under the question, “How might we allow more [tools] to be available?”.        High-level solution directions that rose to the top.    How Might We?... Round Two    We had a clear direction from the advisory panel, but our concepts were very high-level, and we needed to drill into them them further. We did this by taking each concept (e.g., "Tiered SLAs") and expanding through more "how might we?" exercises.        Diagram showing chains of solutions that originate from “How might we allow more tools to be available?”.     Product Vision    Tying the Story Together    Scenario writing    On Thursday, we took our top ideas and wrote scenarios describing a future in which our customers are enjoying the value of our not-yet-existent products. We were envisioning presenting a storyboard to the advisory panel on Friday and wanted our ideas to be approachable and expressive.        Scenario writing session      Our scenario writing didn't work out as planned. We would need to improvise.   A Misfire and Redress    “What is the point of scenario writing? Have we learned anything?”, asked an engineer on Friday morning. I probed the room. “What do we think?”, I asked.       In short, our solution ideas were not solid enough technically for our engineers. For a lot of types of projects, safe assumptions can be made about the tech. That was not the case for us.       We went back to the whiteboard and discussed.

The solution was to group ideas into product concepts. Those allowed the engineers to envision the "how" to a level that was acceptable.   Idea Boards and Advisory Panel Feedback, Round Two    We still needed something to present to the advisory panel. We came up with idea boards. Idea boards describe product concepts, and tie together outcomes, evidence and personas.       We created an idea board for each of our top concepts and presented to the advisory panel for feedback.       Idea board example    INDEPENDENT SERVICE OFFERINGS     Outcome: Operators can delegate the curation and management of services to non-operators, effectively increasing the offerings.      DIFFERENT LEVELS OF SUPPORT     Outcome: Developers can provision a wider variety of tools that are not mission-critical (such as databases with lower uptime) and are managed by non-operators.      AUTOMATED TOOL INTEGRATION TESTS     Outcome: Third-party tool providers have increased confidence in an integration before releasing it. Operators have increased confidence in an integration before managing it.       Storyboard    Externalizing the Product Vision       After ideation week, I worked with marketing and an illustrator to bring the prodcut vision to life.        Conclusion    Design Impact       Design Sprint       Our ideation session was the foundation for a compact, two-day design sprint. (I plan to include the sprint as a separate case study.)       Research Impact       Our research deck got a lot of attention. I presented it several times as part of our integration process. At several points there were dozens of people in the Google doc, including vice presidents. One PM told me she had been referring to the deck on a regular basis. In addition, I later used the research findings to inform two other related projects.       Design Practice Impact       I documented the design process and presented it internally and externally, including two times for the U.K. Government Digital Services.       Learnings    If something is not working, nip it in the bud and pivot: Our initial scenario-based presentation didn't gel. We took the outcome we were after and came up with a solution that worked. High-trust between team members enables efficiency: High trust enables critical feedback, which was necessary for us to recover from mistakes. Transdisciplinary ideation is powerful: We gained insights that only came about because designers, engineers and field engineers were working together on the same problem. Marketing team members on our advisory panel had feedback that the rest of us would not have thought of.

Storyboards are great tools for conveying vision: I got feedback that people had no idea of what we were up to until they saw the storyboard. In large organizations, I've found people will hear about your project, assume worst-case scenario, and imagine conflict that doesn't exist. A good vision storyboard can dispell mistrust and open doors for collaboration.  menu  chevron_left Case Studies HealthWatch Role and Team Roadmapping Research Strategy Collaboration Rapid prototyping Logic design Dog fooding Conclusion Knative Role and Team Strategy Design Strategy Story Mapping Competitive Analysis Ideation Rapid Prototyping Wireframing Alpha Release Conclusion Dev Console Role and Team Roadmapping Research Personas Workshop Prep Problem Prioritization Causal Mapping Solutioning Product Vision Storyboard Conclusion Obama How it works Role and Team Concepting Wireframing People Pivot Development Conclusion     © Copyright 2023 by Kevin Gates.    kgates@gmail.com LinkedIn  .
