Kevin Gates - Knative UI   Kevin Gates       Kevin Gates Case Studies   Projects / Writing      Ask My AI    TL;DR: KNative, a popular open-source tool, automatically frees up unused memory in the cloud, creating big cost savings for IT departments. We created a UX for it that lowers barriers to adoption, enabling those savings to scale. Our collaborative approach to design produced market-differentiating features.    	  Humanizing Open Source 	  	  Starting From the Ground Up 	  	  When a product manager and I kicked off this project, our initial idea was to perform a heuristic evaluation of KNative’s open-source GUI, make a few recommendations, and that would be the end of it. 	  	  Instead, what we found was a UI that had been given little attention. We couldn’t fix it half way—it needed a redesign from the ground up. 	   The Problem 	  Data Model as the UI 	  	  Overall, the open-source UI looked like a reflection of a data model, and did not have well-thought-out flows, hierarchy, or layout. Below are a few examples of problems we found: 	   Data Tables 	   The main interface consisted of four tables which seemed like tables you might find in a database (configurations, status messages, routes, revisions). 	    Disjointed Layout 	   Information was not laid out logically. For example, system status information relevant to the table in the lower left appeared on the upper right. 	    Poor UX Flow 	   The only actions available were a bit of clickable text on the upper left that said "configuration", which led to an editable config file, and a delete button on the upper right. 	     Role and Team My Role 	  Over a period of six weeks, I drove the overall approach to design as the design lead. I worked closely with a PM in planning workshops, conducting user interviews, and rapid prototyping. 	  SKILLS 	  Design strategy, competitive analysis, collaborative ideation, rapid prototyping, usability testing 	  COLLABORATORS 	  On a daily basis, I worked with a product manager and engineering lead. Two visual designers rotated onto the project towards the end. 	   Strategy 	  Assessing Risk in the World of Open Source 	   	   We were building on open-source technology that had already survived contact with reality. That meant the functionality had already been proven useful, but we still needed to figure out what value-add we could bring.

...see moreexpand_more  	   Demand 	   	   Demand for serverless technology is well-established and growing. According to the 2020 Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF) survey: 	   30% of organizations polled have production apps running on serverless and another 35% are considering it. The CNCF "fully expect growth to continue". 82% of organizations who are using serverless are using hosted solutions.  	   Is your organization using serverless? 	     	   How is your organization using serverless technology? 	     	   VALUE 	   	   KNative has been validated in the marketplace and demonstrably solves well-known IT problems: 	   Resource efficiency: Serverless automatically frees up unused memory and CPU allocations in the cloud (“scales to zero”). Emergency recovery: Serverless automatically creates application backups. Traffic splitting: Serverless makes it easy to split traffic between versions for A/B testing and gradual rollouts.   Design Strategy Targeting Users By Skill Level 	   Near the start of the design process, I like the team to imagine it's two years after launch and the product is very successful. I then ask, "Who is the average user? What are they like?" 	   ...see moreexpand_more 	  From previous research, we knew a few things relevant to our project: 	  Most large-scale enterprises, 97 to 99% of developers are early-career and don’t have skills in managing and configuring cloud-based infrastructure. Many developers at large organizations are contractors (up to 50%) who often don't have direct access to the cloud platform and rely on GUIs. Some customers have elite engineering teams who use commandline tools and automate processes. 	  Our design strategy took this into account: We assumed most developers will be early-career and new to serverless, but will gain skill over time. 	    	  This matrix shows how we targeted users by skill level, and who by definition should be the largest user cohort. 	    Story Mapping Laying Out a Goal-Directed Narrative 	   The PM had created story maps by analyzing the open-source functionality, which did a good job at outlining features and aligning them with outcomes. 	   ...see moreexpand_more 	  The problem is they didn't capture the narrative of the experience. User experiences are a series of activities that take place in the physical world in service of people achieving goals, which are often interconnected in logical ways.

Story Map Workshop 	  	  I facilitated a workshop in which we deconstructed the story maps and created a narrative in the form of what I call a proto-journey map. 	    	  Our proto-journey map layed out a flow centered on user goals. 	   	  First and foremost, we wanted to have a foundation for ideation sessions in which we rethink the journey. In addition, we wanted to: 	  Create a narrative which allow us to see the big picture and how tasks are connected and in service of the user reaching goals. Have a way to fold in and organize other materials such as research findings and competitive analysis.  	  Anatomy of an experience map. Stories were deconstructed and arranged a linear narrative with tasks, goals and milestones. 	    Competitive Analysis Understanding the Competition 	   We took screenshots of our competitor's UIs and pasted them along our journey map, aligning them to user tasks. Our goal was to make sure we had parity with Google, IBM and the OS version, and to find inspiration in things they do well. 	   ...see moreexpand_more   	  Screenshots of our competitor's UIs were aligned with user goals (left). Miro's zoom feature allowed us to zoom into screenshots and see details. 	   COMPETITOR STRENGTHS We liked Google’s stepper approach to creating and updating serverless apps and contextual help. RedHat had advanced features we liked, such as allowing more experienced users to paste configurations into the UI rather than fill out a form.  COMPETITOR WEAKNESSES Most UIs reflected data structures, not user mental models. Elements in UI not logically grouped together. Poor / confusing wayfinding. System status messages incoherent. Lack of guided step-by-step flows or beginners (“Paste YAML here”).    Ideation Sketching a Differentiating UX 	   Now that we understood something about our competitors and we had a model of the user journey, we set up an ideation workshop (a.k.a., design studio) in which everyone (engineers, field engineers, design and PM) would sketch concepts. The goal was to get diverse perspectives to seed our creative process. 	   ...see moreexpand_more  	  Market Differentiators 	  	  We dot-voted sketches and discussed what we liked. A few solved key user problems in creative ways, and had the potential to differentiate us from competitors.

Intuitive Layout 	   Several people came up with similar designs that merged information from multiple sources into intuitive tables. Many had a visual representation of status. 	    Perfomance Analysis 	   Serverless technology will automatically free up system resources when an app has no traffic. When traffic returns, starting up cold can be slow. Exercising the scaling functionality, then showing how long cold starts take would enable developers to optimize for more responsiveness. 	    Change Log 	   Changes to apps (revisions) are described in YAML. This feature would create a diff between revisions, allowing developers to see exactly what changed. This could be an interesting place to start debugging. 	     Rapid Prototyping Iterative Testing and Evaluation 	   UX design for cloud computing requires navigating a lot of complexity. I've found an iterative approach with constant feedback is the best way to work through it. 	   ...see moreexpand_more  	   Setting Up User Feedback 	   	   We set feedback from many types of users: experts, novices, field engineers, internal engineers. For each of these, we had two types of learning goals: 	   Evaluative (a.k.a., usability): Can the user complete given tasks? What about developers with no experience with serverless, can they understand everything? Generative: When do developers use GUIs versus command lines? Why do they use them? What would they change in our UI, if anything? 	   Prototyping and Testing 	   	   With the PM and I largely aligned on our approach, I created our first prototype based on our key user scenarios. 	     	  Our usability tests had three primary user scenarios: Create, update and route traffic. We synthesized results directly on the wire frames to maintain context. 	   	  Key Findings 	  Big frustrations were hidden in the details: Our first versions did not have default settings for memory allocations. The problem is that when the user is filling this field out, their app is likely in its early stages, and they would have no idea how much memory they would need at launch. This was a required field, so when you put everything together, we were forcing the user to answer a question they could not answer. Novice users were confused: A big part of the confusion was caused by a few terms associated with serverless technology that were overloaded (in particular, "service" and "traffic"). One tester thought he was configuring a database for the first 10 minutes of the interview.

GUIs can provide value for all users: GUIs can provide value for all users: GUIs can provide value for new users, early-career developers, seasoned veterans, and even expert users. This reinforced our initial assumptions.   Wireframing Aligning Competing Forces 	   After several iterations and feedback sessions, our wireframes started to feel more and more solid. There were several forces aligning in our designs: 	   ...see moreexpand_more  Business strategy: Lower barriers to adoption Design strategy: Target users by skill level Harmony: Stay true to KNative conventions User experience: Use well-known UX patterns 	   Sometimes these forces were complementary; sometimes they were competing. Below are examples of how they manifest themselves after many iterations and feedback sessions: 	     	   Contextual help supported all of our objectives. 	    	   Keeping open-sources terminology is important, especially when users go between the UI and command line. 	     	   We merged version information into a single table and included visual icons to represent status. 	    	   Our UI shows diffs between versions. The UI uses source control conventions well-known to developers. 	     Alpha Release A UX to Lower Barriers to Adoption and Enable Scale 	  Key Value Props 	  Users don't have to think about infrastructure: We aimed to allow users with no knowledge of Kubernetes or KNative to easily access their benefits. Features for all skill levels: We designed for early-career developers, but also included features that allow for more efficient workflows for our advanced users. Cost savings at scale: KNative manages memory allocation automatically which results in big cost savings. Making KNative easily accessible to developers allows those savings to scale.   	   Early design for our alpha release 	    	   Early design for our alpha release 	     Conclusion 	  Impact 	  	  Initial Feedback From the Field 	  	  As of this writing, the project was going into an internal alpha release. Initial feedback from our field team was very positive. “When a developer doesn’t know much about a new technology, just clicking a box is powerful.” was a response from a field engineer in EMEA. 	  	  Differentiating Features 	  	  Our design studio produced two features that our competitors do not have: change logs and scaling analysis. These features address the top two pain points for serverless technology: debugging and cold starts (according to the Serverless Community Survey).

Impact on the Design Practice 	  	  Our "target skill level" matrix went over well with other design teams, and was adopted for other projects. 	  	  Learnings 	  Opinionated APIs can be challenging: We designed a UI on top of two very opinionated APIs (KNative and Kubernetes) and those opinions were often at odds with good UX principles. Putting competitive analysis in the journey map worked: We found ourselves referring back to the analysis often, and I got feedback that others were doing the same.  menu  chevron_left Case Studies HealthWatch Role and Team Roadmapping Research Strategy Collaboration Rapid prototyping Logic design Dog fooding Conclusion Knative Role and Team Strategy Design Strategy Story Mapping Competitive Analysis Ideation Rapid Prototyping Wireframing Alpha Release Conclusion Dev Console Role and Team Roadmapping Research Personas Workshop Prep Problem Prioritization Causal Mapping Solutioning Product Vision Storyboard Conclusion Obama How it works Role and Team Concepting Wireframing People Pivot Development Conclusion     © Copyright 2023 by Kevin Gates.    kgates@gmail.com LinkedIn  .

olio app.herokuapp.com healthwatch?sn=1.  Kevin Gates - HealthWatch   Kevin Gates       Kevin Gates Case Studies   Projects / Writing      Ask My AI    TL;DR: HealthWatch embodies cloud monitoring best practices out of the box, enabling IT teams to get up and running quickly.   It gained 100% adoption with 32 Fortune 500 companies we tracked. To accomplish this, we embedded domain experts in the design process, which paid off in spades.       The Road to 100% Customer Adoption      Starting Off on a “Fool’s Errand”      This project did not start well. During an early stakeholder interview, a marketing director described the concept behind HealthWatch as a "fool’s errand”.      Not long before, the PM and I had been tasked with creating an out-of-the-box dashboard for monitoring Pivotal Cloud Foundry, an operating system for cloud platforms like Amazon’s AWS. Neither of us thought it was a viable concept—the market we were entering was crowded with entrenched players.     HealthWatch being unveiled by Pivotal’s VP of R&D at a conference in San Francisco.     A Value-Driven Design       Fast forward several months. Despite our initial misgivings, we found a direction that would go on to gain 100% adoption with 30+ Fortune 500 companies we tracked.       The road getting to a viable product was not easy. It involved a highly iterative design process. Low fidelity, co-creation and rapid prototyping were part of our daily lives.    Key value props: Baked-in expertise: All of our designs were informed by cloud monitoring experts with deep domain knowledge. Batteries included: HealthWatch comes ready to run out-of-the-box. Customers immediately get access to dashboards that embody best practices without having to configure anything.   The main dashboard. With 2,600 metrics emitted by the platform, our design process allowed us to suss out what 30 to 40 would reliably convey system-wide health. (Visual design by Justin Roozeboom.)    Users often ask themselves, "What happened while I was gone?". Deep-dive views show historical data. (Visual design by Justin Roozeboom.)    A historical view of automated test results. (Visual design by Justin Roozeboom.)  Role and Team My Role   As part of an agile team, I drove the overall design process for about a year.

I shared research responsibilities with the PM, and led co-creation, rapid prototyping, usability testing and validation.   Skills   Concept validation, user research, data dashboard design, co-creation, rapid prototyping, dogfooding   Collaborators   I worked closely with the PM at first, as I was the sole designer for the first several months. Later on, two designers joined the project on temporary rotations. By enlarge, I worked remotely from San Francisco, while the rest of the team was based in Denver.    Roadmapping   Choosing the Right Design Tools      At the onset, we had a lot of questions and not a lot of time. I was meeting with the PM everyday, and during our discussions we started capturing questions we were asking ourselves.    ...see moreexpand_more     We had big questions like, “Is the product viable?” and, “Should we base our product on an open source solution?” The roadmap helped us agree on priorities and map them out on a calendar, which helped us manage stakeholder expectations.     Our design roadmap made clear what we were doing and why.  Our roadmap calendar shows timelines.   Research   Understanding Our Place in the Ecosystem      When we kicked things off, our top concern was that we would need to displace entrenched products in order to be successful. With that in mind, we created a topic map and set up interviews with around 30 customer platform operators and Pivotal field engineers (who work onsite at our customers’ offices).    ...see moreexpand_more Research Goals Validation of an out-of-the-box GUI: Would customers even use our GUI? Our top concern was that we would have to displace entrenched competitors in order for our GUI to get traction. Understanding the reality of customer platform monitoring: How do customers currently monitor their platforms? What works well? What is painful?  The PM and I created an affinity map together in which we grouped findings into themes, such as “product-market fit” and “learning process”.  Key Findings    After a few weeks of user interviews and synthesis, a few themes surfaced to the top that we felt addressed our concerns:    Third-party tools are hard to learn: Third-party tools are sophisticated and take time to learn. This can be a burden for customers who are new to Cloud Foundry—several customers had tried to use third-party tools for platform monitoring, but gave up.

Data overload: There are thousands of metrics and customers don’t know what to monitor. Only a relatively small number of metrics are really needed to be effective, the trick is to know which ones. Platform monitoring dashboards are "snowflakes": In the long run, customer reporting needs are unique and operations teams will create dashboards that fit their needs.    “The last thing we want is the end user telling us about problems with our applications.” - Platform operator   Target Persona: New Operators    Our target users were platform operators who were new to monitoring. Despite that their teams tend to be small, with four to ten members, there can be more than a thousand developers using the platform once it is at scale.    Frustrations Data overload—There is too much data and it's difficult to make sense of Platform-monitoring software is difficult to learn Flying blind—Doesn't know about issues until there is an outage  Quotes “The last thing we want is the end user telling us about problems with our applications.” "Platform issues are almost always caused by humans, and it's almost always because someone was doing an update."   A Non-linear Journey    Our experience map captured that we would be part of a larger ecosystem: When there is a potential issue with the platform, our users go on a journey through a constellation of alerts, dashboards, and Slack conversations.       Our work would manifest as touchpoints along that journey—sometimes as UIs, sometimes not.     The journey map for HealthWatch was non-linear  Strategy   Giving Users a Bridge to Mastery      Around this time, we started thinking about how we might fit in the market: Advanced users will just use the API, but those new to the platform might benefit from an opinionated GUI that would be a bridge for them as they got up and running.    ...see moreexpand_more  Define KPIs: A lot of monitoring know-how was in the heads of our field and R&D engineers. The PM consolidated and documented this know-how in the form of KPIs. Provide an API: We knew that our customers would eventually create custom solutions, so we made our metrics available by API. Design a GUI: We used the KPIs as a basis for a UI that would be exemplar of how to visualize and organize metrics. This ended up being a two-way street as some of our designs defined KPIs.

Design Posture    As new users gain skill, they should graduate to creating their own dashboards using the API.      Collaboration    Making Feedback Continuous       Soon after we started rapid prototyping, we realized that we faced a seemingly insurmountable task: We needed to become experts in a complex domain in order to help users become experts in the domain.    ...see moreexpand_more Expert Advisory Panel    We reached out through our networks and found coworkers with deep knowledge of platform monitoring and fully embedded them in the design process.    A session with the expert advisory panel.    We made feedback a forcing function. This is how it worked: I committed to presenting to the panel whatever I was working on, in whatever state it was in, every Friday morning.        It worked well—In addition to getting continuous feedback, we built our own education into the process.       We faced a seemingly insurmountable task: We needed to become experts in a complex domain in order to help users become experts in the domain.   Co-creation    We had Cloud Foundry operators walk us through a time when they had to troubleshoot a problem. We then went to a whiteboard and co-designed the dashboard the operator wished they had.    Co-creation brought deep domain expertise to our understanding of the problems we were solving.     Here are a couple of interesting things we learned:    The question “When?” is paramount: “When did the system first start to have problems?” is one of the first things operators want to know. We made timestamps as precise as possible. Component IDs and timestamps are keys: Operators bounce between our app and others, such as log search tools. Component IDs and timestamps are the two things you need when jumping between systems. We add copy-to-clipboard buttons to everything we could. Weekly Engineering Reviews    Every Monday, I got the whole team together for 30 minutes and present whatever designs I was working on: low-fi sketches, user flows, wireframes, UI components, etc. I then got the engineering team to offer any thoughts or concerns they might have.    Cost vs. User Value    By putting our in-progress designs on the table for engineering every week, we got an early gut-check for complexity. This got the value/cost discussion going early and meant we could react to complexity with intention.      No-brainers    Low-cost, high-value features can go into the backlog.

Zone of Excess Compexity    Some features have user value, but are costly. Design should collaborate with engineering to lower costs.     Required or Differentiating    High-cost, high-value features are either must-be's (the product doesn't work without them), or market differentiators.      Rapid Prototyping   Rapid Prototyping Example: HTTP Requests      In our situation, it was almost impossible to thoroughly understand metrics and all their implications up front. We needed to just get ideas out of our heads and into the world quickly and start learning. Rapid prototyping means being wrong constantly until you gain confidence that you aren't.     Evolution of a metrics panel.    The design above on the left was our initial attempt at a router health design we presented to the advisory panel. It did not go over well. During our first feedback session, one panel member's first comment was, "I don't like anything about this."    Our first router panel design didn't go over well.   I probed the panel by asking, "What about this don't you like?" We quickly learned what we got wrong: We focused too much on router CPU and tried to force everything into a stoplight visualization. We didn't include overall traffic. So we went back to the drawing board. (Literally.)    Two more versions. We increased fidelity as our confidence in the design grew.   What we learned from the expert panel is that operators care first about overall traffic and whether it is trending up or down. If something is concerning, they then will want router details, such as latency and errors.      Layout and Hierarchy      For data dashboards, like in typography, meaning drives layout.      Through our iterative design process, we first understood the implications of metrics and how they naturally hung together with others. That drove layout decisions. For example, some indicators, like HTTP throughput, are key to interpreting others, and therefore are given prominence.      Interpreting a metric is often contingent on others. This informed layout decisions.  Logic Design    What Goes On Behind the Scenes for a Dashboard Stoplight       The simplicity of our CLI command stoplights belies the complex logic behind them. Cloud Foundry enables developers to push code onto the cloud easily, but behind this functionality are several systems all of which emit hundreds of metrics.    ...see moreexpand_more     Simplicity and Reliability        Rather than burdening users with interpreting these, HealthWatch provides an automated battery of tests.

The system automatically creates a minimal-footprint app, pushes it to the cloud, then tests it's health.          Everything is healthy.          Potential problem.          Users need to investigate.         Logic design    Each test (push, start, stop, etc.) are treated independentaly. For each suite of tests, individual stoplight error states are determined given the available information we have for each. For an isolated failure, the stoplight will be yellow. For sequnetial faillures (e.g., two push failure in a row), the stoplight will be red. Test that don't run are not counted. If all test do not run for a stoplight, stoplight will be gray.      Logic diagrams show test results (top rows) and how the results appear on the dashboard. This example shows how a self-correcting system can result in failed tests without displaying red stop lights.      Dogfooding    Making First Contact with Reality       HealthWatch would need to help operations teams navigate through unexpected disruptions, the nature of which can't be predicted. That meant we needed to test under real-world circumstances with real data.         HealthWatch in the wild. Our ops team in Dublin installed and used our alpha version and gave us weekly feedback.       Pivotal’s cloud operations team in Ireland agreed to be the first to use HealthWatch in a production environment. That team hosted a SaaS product called Pivotal Tracker, an agile project management tool which has several hundred-thousand active users.       Weekly Check-Ins       We conducted weekly check-in sessions. In these sessions, members of the operations teams walked us through scenarios where they used HealthWatch. We got a lot of usability feedback, but also validated that the app helped them get through unexpected problems, such as failed upgrades.       Card Sorting       Once the operations team had been using HealthWatch for several weeks, we gave operations team members workbooks in Google Draw that contained movable metrics panels. We then asked them to arrange the dashboard the way they would like it.       After that, we interviewed the operators to understand the thinking behind their layouts. This eventually led us to grouping metrics into three areas of impact.        After using the app for a few weeks, alpha testers arranged their ideal dashboard layout.     END-USER IMPACT     End users affected. Generally urgent.     DEVELOPER IMPACT     Not as urgent as end-user impact, but potentially important.

PLATFORM IMPACT     Underlying VMs and other platform infrastructure.         Feature Vetting       After the card sort, we had team members independently tell us what metrics they would remove from the main dashboard and why. This got us to declutter and move about 25 percent of the main dashboard to deep dive views.        Alpha testers voted metrics off the island.     Conclusion    HealthWatch's Impact       Measurable Outcomes       We hypothesized that five of seven potential customers would install our closed beta and give us three feedback sessions. This tested whether customers would be willing to "pay" for beta access and worked as a leading indicator of adoption. The first five signed up.       Customer Impact       In one of our beta feedback sessions, a customer kept repeating the phrase, "This is golden". A few weeks before, his team started hearing complaints from developers that they couldn't push their apps onto the platform.       What nobody knew at the time was that the platform had run out of memory. That resulted in days of frustration that would not have occurred if they had our product—the team would have been alerted to low memory long before running out.       U.S. Patent       About one year after our GA (generally available) release, I shared a U.S. patent with the PM and engineering lead.       Diagram from patent No. US20190188107A1 for “Health monitoring for cloud computing platforms”.       Impact on Pivotal's Design Practice       User-centered design was not widely practiced in Pivotal's R&D department when we started. After launch, I gave two lightning talks and wrote an essay on our process (which was published in PC World and translated into German).       Impact on Marketing and Sales       Dogfooding allowed for our sales and marketing teams to say, “We use this product ourselves.” I also heard from the field that the out-of-the-box UI played well in sales demos.     menu  chevron_left Case Studies HealthWatch Role and Team Roadmapping Research Strategy Collaboration Rapid prototyping Logic design Dog fooding Conclusion Knative Role and Team Strategy Design Strategy Story Mapping Competitive Analysis Ideation Rapid Prototyping Wireframing Alpha Release Conclusion Dev Console Role and Team Roadmapping Research Personas Workshop Prep Problem Prioritization Causal Mapping Solutioning Product Vision Storyboard Conclusion Obama How it works Role and Team Concepting Wireframing People Pivot Development Conclusion     © Copyright 2023 by Kevin Gates.    kgates@gmail.com LinkedIn  .

Kevin Gates - Obama   Kevin Gates       Kevin Gates Case Studies   Projects / Writing      Ask My AI    TL;DR: Project Atlas is an app that highlights the Obama campaign's main themes, all taylored to voter location. It launched on the president's twitter feed and reached his 42 million followers. To get to the finishline, I had to keep an all-volunteer tech team motivated while performing a hard pivot.      Getting Creative Under a Hard Deadline      A Vague Idea and a Looming Election      In June of 2012, I was given a big data set about President Obama's achievements, but not much more than a vague direction: make something voter-facing, and make it location-based.      The election was four months away and we had no idea where we would end up. This is what we came up with...     Project Atlas detects the user's location (in this case Orange County, Florida) and shows Obama's achievements, all taylored to be as local as possible.  How it works Geo-targeting Obama's Achievements   After exploring several concepts, pursuing a direction, pivoting mid-project, we came up with a solution and launched five weeks before election day. This is how it works:   STEP ONE   A voter gets a tweet from Obama inviting them to check out a map. They click it.       arrow_downward    STEP TWO   The app detects the voter's location. In this case they are in Orange County, Florida.       arrow_downward    STEP THREE   The app compiles a webpage about Obama's achievements, all tailored to Orange County, Florida.     Role and Team My Role   Based in the San Francisco Technology Field Office, I led the ideation, wireframing and ran weekly presentations to campaign headquarters. In addition, I led the dev team, wrangled data, coordinated the efforts of volunteers, and wrote a good portion of the JavaScript and CSS.   SKILLS   Concept exploration, geospatial analysis (PostGIS, QuantumGIS), wireframing, JavaScript, Google Maps API   COLLABORATORS   I worked with a director of product management in the Chicago headquarters. Two engineers volunteered 8 - 16 hours a week, a dozen volunteers helped wrangle data, and the visual design was polished by the branding team.

Conceptiing    Exploring the Data With Geospatial Tools       Research from campaign headquarters revealed that voters respond more favorably to Obama's achievements when they are made relevant to them. With that in mind, I set out to better understand the shape of the data and what might be done with it. My goal was to come up with ideas to pitch to our stakeholders that would resonate with the campaign's goals.             I set up geospatial tools (PostgreSQL, PostGIS and QuantumGIS) and started exploring the data. I considered a lot of possibilities: children gaining health care; renewable energy investments; tax savings, and many more.       Examples of maps I created to explore the campaign's data sets using PostgreSQL, PostGIS and QuantumGIS.     Wireframing    Refining a Direction       Our stakeholders liked the direction of a recovery-themed map I created, which used data about the 2009 stimulus bill. We got the go-ahead to move forward.       The first wireframes showed stimulus projects color-coded by category. These categories corresponded to the campaign's core policy areas: education, energy, health and infrastructure.       Using Google's mapping API, we could detect the users location, and just show data points from their area.         The first wireframes I created show the top five recovery projects per state. Projects are color-coded to correspond to campaign themes: education, energy, health and infrastructure. The maps were created using real data.         As the user zooms in, more and more projects are revealed. At each zoom level, the most relevant projects are highlighted in the inset at the left.      People    Inspiring Volunteers       With a green light from headquarters, momentum started building. We would need engineers and since most of the data was uncategorized, we would need volunteers to help categorize data.    ...see moreexpand_more    The question lingered: How could we motivate an all-volunteer team to hit hard deadlines? Here are a few approaches:    Assume intrinsic motivation: The engineers who volunteered were willing to go through an interview process, so they likely want to help the president get re-elected. Act like shipping product is inevitable: If it looks like we are going to ship, engineers will be more likely to help. Momentum then builds in a virtuous cycle. Be clear about scope: Break work down into doable chunks. Be sure volunteers know exactly what is expected of them and when things need to be done.

The question lingered: How could we motivate an all-volunteer team to hit hard deadlines?     Pivot    Adapting to Chaos       In July, things abruptly changed for the worse. Just as the recovery map concept started to take shape, the Romney campaign attacked the stimulus bill. The Obama campaign banned the mention of it. The project was stopped in its tracks.    ...see moreexpand_more A New Direction    Everything could have easily ended at this point. Rather than give up, I went back and re-evaluated the data sets we hadn't used, and came up with a new solution.       The new concept detected the user's location and displayed the president's accomplishments across all of the campaign's policy areas. The digital director at headquarters liked the new direction, and gave us the green light.      Campaign headquarters put the kibosh on our project. We were stopped in our tracks.       Early version of our new direction. This concept pulls in relevant data about health, education, transportation etc.     Development    Hitting a Tight Deadline       Due to time constraints, I stopped wire framing and worked directly with the prototype we had started. Our goal was to have a fully functional app with just enough opinion about the UI. The branding team in Chicago would take it from there.    Mobile-first    We wanted to make sure the app would work well on mobile. As we wrote the code and defined the UI, we tested constantly on several phones and tablets.        Using a responsive UI framework, I was able to simultaneously design for tablets and phones, helping us keep to a very tight timeline.        An early fully-functioning version.      The final version of the app after the branding team's work.    Conclusion   Impact: Reaching Millions of Voters      The final weeks of the campaign were total chaos. Despite this, we managed to launch the morning of October 1st. Later that day, the campaign sent the URL out on Obama's Twitter account, where it reached the president's 42 million followers.
